name: Microprocessor
tagline: A CPU implemented as an integrated circuit, enabling personal and embedded computing
problem:
General-purpose computing was once room-sized and rare. Civilization needs computation everywhere: control systems, communications, instrumentation, and eventually personal devices.

Microprocessors compress a CPU into a single chip, reducing cost and power while enabling mass deployment of programmable systems.

The remaining problem for Microprocessor is operational reliability. A reader needs more than a name and a historical note: they need to know what failure this capability prevents, what resources it consumes, what signs show that it is failing, and when it is safer to stop than to improvise. This entry therefore treats microprocessor as a teachable system with inputs, inspection points, records, and limits rather than as a single trick.
overview:
A microprocessor integrates arithmetic logic, registers, control, and interfaces. It executes instructions from memory and interacts with peripherals via buses.

The key to bootstrap is modularity: even if you cannot manufacture microprocessors locally, you can build robust systems around imported chips if you understand their constraints, power needs, and failure modes.

In collapse recovery, microprocessor must be repairable with local materials, teachable to more than one person, and documented well enough that the method survives injury, migration, or leadership change. In a space-settlement context, the same capability must account for closed-loop resources, contamination control, spare-part scarcity, power budgets, and the cost of mistakes in a sealed habitat. The practical minimum is a controlled trial, written checks, safe storage, and review before scale-up.

principles.name: Instruction execution
principles.explanation: Machine code drives a predictable state machine.

principles.name: Memory hierarchy
principles.explanation: Caches and memory buses trade speed for capacity.

principles.name: Clocking and timing
principles.explanation: Synchronous design requires stable clock distribution and timing margins.

components.name: Use-case boundary
components.function: Defines what Microprocessor is meant to accomplish and what it must not be used for
components.madeFrom: Written requirements, hazard notes, local constraints, and responsible operator assignment
components.criticalNote: A vague use case leads to unsafe improvisation.

components.name: Input control point
components.function: Checks materials, tools, information, and environmental conditions before work starts
components.position: At receiving, storage, or setup area
components.madeFrom: Labels, inspection notes, gauges, samples, and quarantine bins
components.criticalNote: Unknown inputs should be isolated until checked.

rawMaterials.name: Electric power
rawMaterials.purpose: Energy for computation, storage, and networking
earthLocations: Electrical grids globally; generators in remote sites
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Solar + batteries is the simplest early-space pathway; plan for tight energy budgets and thermal rejection.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Stable voltage regulation, grounding, and basic power-quality control.

rawMaterials.name: Semiconductor devices
rawMaterials.purpose: Transistors and ICs that perform switching, storage, and signal conditioning
earthLocations: Global semiconductor supply chains; packaged IC distributors
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Early colonies import packaged devices; long-term local manufacture requires clean chemistry and crystal growth capabilities.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: ESD-safe handling; correct packaging, heat sinking, and derating for temperature and radiation.

rawMaterials.name: Documentation and standards
rawMaterials.purpose: Make designs reproducible and debuggable across teams and time
earthLocations: Libraries, universities, standards bodies, archived web
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Mirror core references offline; maintain printed and digital copies with checksums and redundancy.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Versioning, change control, and clear measurement units and assumptions.

rawMaterials.name: Printed circuit boards (PCBs)
rawMaterials.purpose: Mount and interconnect processors, memory, and peripherals
rawMaterials.spaceAlternatives: Local PCB fab is plausible with chemicals and precision; early off-world relies on imported boards and careful repair.
rawMaterials.processingRequired: Copper etching/lamination, soldering, inspection, and rework capability.

buildSteps.title: Select a processor and document constraints
buildSteps.description: Pick an architecture; record voltage, clocking, and I/O requirements.

buildSteps.title: Design stable power and clocking
buildSteps.description: Add regulators, decoupling, and clock sources per reference designs.
buildSteps.warningNote: Power integrity issues cause intermittent faults.

buildSteps.title: Bring up minimal firmware
buildSteps.description: Boot a simple monitor; verify memory and I/O stepwise.
buildSteps.warningNote: Complex software before hardware validation wastes time.

buildSteps.title: Add peripherals and tests
buildSteps.description: Integrate storage/networking; add self-tests and logs.

buildSteps.title: Define the local purpose and stop conditions
buildSteps.description: Write down why Microprocessor is needed, who is responsible, what success looks like, and which conditions require stopping or escalation.
buildSteps.warningNote: Do not start a life-critical or infrastructure-critical use without clear stop conditions.

history.event: Early microprocessors enable embedded and personal computing.
Energy efficiency and integration dominate design.

impact:
Microprocessors make computation ubiquitous and enable automation, communication, and measurement across society.

They also deepen dependencies on semiconductor manufacturing; The Codex treats them as a node that can be imported and maintained even if not locally fabricated.

images.caption: Instruction fetch, registers, ALU, bus, peripherals

sources.title: Hennessy & Patterson — Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach
